summary: Stancil argues that natural language is a poor substitute for code when doing nuanced analysis, so LLMs shouldn’t directly write SQL for complex business questions. Instead, he sees AI as most valuable when it sits atop BI models or helps translate rough, mixed SQL/English sketches into precise queries, preserving guardrails and precision.
money_quotes: The obvious point here is that natural language isn’t always a particularly useful way to create something.
For certain types of output, English is crude, imprecise, and painfully inefficient.
There are thousands of computational devils in details like how to handle nulls.
I want to use a bad query to help me write a good query.
LLMs as an analyst, turning novel business questions into full queries? Seems unwise.
key_insight: LLMs should augment analysts by tightening rough SQL or navigating BI models, not replace precise, code-based analysis with English prompts.
